sentences.text: I would like to add that before the first action on the spring of '42 in [INAUDIBLE], Rowne had an action.
It was a massacre.
An enormous amount of Jewish people perished.
I had family there.
I had cousins, I had aunts, and they all perished.
You know, rumors there.
And we had mainly from the people that survived.
They came to Hoszcza.
They came to hide in Hoszcza, to run away from Rowne, in case it happens again.
And when it did happen, the second time, the action in the fall, those that ran away from other towns perished in our town also.
By that time, the rumors were coming that in the fall something will happen.
Because we heard that each town had one and had a follow-up, another one.
So we knew that our turn will come.
And my sister, you know, my mother secured a place for her in a far away village.
That village had really no homes but one big farm, far away from each other.
And we thought it an ideal place to hide.
At first, she worked a little bit for them, doing chores and everything.
Right, sister Leah.
But later, she had to hide.
And I-- at that time, as you know, as I survived the first action with my mother, I was sure that we'll be doing the same thing at the second action.
But she said, no.
She said, she's staying in town, and I should go to our trusted maid, Marina, in Boczanica.
So I walked there.
It was-- it was not as I was saying already goodbye for sure, but probably, with a heavy heart and really thinking that I might not see her again.
Which was true.
And she remained in the town.
When I was there at my maid's home-- I could not stay in her house.
She lived across from a small little forest and more like a pasture.
And that's where she wanted me to be.
If I am to keep you, you have to be there during the day.
At night, you'll come into the barn to sleep.
And she would bring me food.
In the pasture, it was very much in the open, because she lived far away from other homes.
She had no reason to be afraid, to keep me even at home.
Because each farmer was far away from each other.
That was the latest in Poland, that the farmers bought a lot of land, owned a lot of land, and were not close to each other.
Because when they were close, and their roofs were out of straw, the fires would just devastate the village.
So they figured out, and they built a more substantial homes.
And fires did subside.
They said-- their reasoning was that if she was found out to shelter Jewish girls, Jewish people, she would be killed.
The rumors were there for a reason, so none of the Ukrainians would shelter the Jewish people.
I stayed there for about two to four-- two to maybe three weeks.

And she was bringing me news from the town of Hoszcza, that the action did occur, that she does not know where my mother is, and that they have finished with the action, and everyone is safe to go back.
I knew.
In the fall in September of '42.
But I knew that that wasn't true.
And when she said she can't keep me anymore, I walked to the town of Hoszcza.
Not to the town, but on the outskirts of the town, where one of our maids-- a previous one, maybe for 20 years she was with us.
Her name was Jurina-- Jurina.
And she was childless, and I was her favorite of all our family.
And still she would not keep me.
She would not shelter me.
And she said that she did not hear from my mother or from my sister.
And I was there hiding for a short while, but not for too long.
Then I left for the villages that were the opposite direction where I was hiding up until now, closer to the town where I was later to survive.
And that was a custom of ours, an extremely trusted custom of very rich but Communist sympathizers, which later became a deputy in the Soviet government after the war.
[INAUDIBLE] was her name.
That's the only last name I remember.
I do not remember the last name of that first maid, Marina, or Jurina even.
I also don't remember-- but her name I happen to remember.
And she was very nice.
I feel that if I would've come with my mother, the relations would have been different.
Very possible that that would have helped them to-- you know, to decide and maybe to keep us.
Because she was such a-- they were all trusted customers, and they were like family, I would say.
So were the maids.
But they would have had more respect than a young little skinny Jewish girl with long braids and a peasant outfit just coming in asking for shelter.
They would have thought maybe my mother has some [INAUDIBLE], because maybe my mother has some possessions.
They said no, they couldn't.
On the way, I met the Pole, the one that I survived at.
And he said that he's looking for a nanny.
He has--
No.
Did you have any papers at this point?
No No papers.
The papers that I had during the German occupation, I did not take with me.
Because on it, it would say [INAUDIBLE].
So I-- What about the star?
Well, very easily I would take it off.
I took it off all through my trips.
They were all by foot.
I knew very well the town-- not the towns, the villages I knew very well.
And I knew the distances, and I knew where to get.
Very possible because I probably traveled with my mother or with someone by horse and buggy to those places.
And I guess I remember.
And besides, I had-- they had all over signs saying what village it is and how many kilometers to get there.
This Pole was-- that was the man that approached me, and he said, where are you going?
I said, I'm just going to visit and see my sister.
And, of course, I didn't tell him that my sister is-- and I am Jewish, or that my sister is Jewish.
But I did stop off to see her before I went to the [INAUDIBLE].
And she was hiding, and she was very, very happy to see me.
And she begged the farmers to keep me, but they said no.
My mother, I told her, is not alive.
I assume she's not alive.
And we both cried.
And I did not want to stay there because I was afraid that she, too, might lose her place of hiding.
And I left.
And I told her that I met this guy, the Pole, and that he offered me to be a nanny to his children.
And she said, take it.
By all means, take it.
Well, it was halfway already to [INAUDIBLE], which is like 18 kilometers from Hoszcza.
And I was halfway in the town of [INAUDIBLE].
So I easily got there.
And on the outskirts was the home of the Pole farmer, also a blacksmith.
He had a very small farm.
He had a wife.
And the wife's family lived in the same house.
It was like an extended family that had an apartment for themselves.
Her father, mother, two brothers and a young sister.
That was [INAUDIBLE].
The Pole's name, my boss, was [INAUDIBLE] , Kaspierkawicz, his wife, [INAUDIBLE] Kaspierkawicz.
Their little ones, three-year-old was [INAUDIBLE], which later was named [?
Alexandre. ?] And the youngest one was a chubby little fellow, [?
Uzik. ?] I doubled up in two when I had to lift him.
And he-- my--
Yes.
I did not tell them I was Jewish.
I told them that my name is Hanka.
I cannot recall the last name I gave him, which I'm sure I did.
That I come not from the Polish parts, but from the Soviet parts of Ukraine.
And the reason I speak Polish is because I stayed with Polish people in the past.
My Polish was way much better than their Polish.
They always mixed Polish and Ukrainian.
And they told me that they would like me to be a nanny for their children, but it turned out to be more so than a nanny.
I did everything-- laundry, and cleaning, and taking the cow to pastures, and taking out the manure from under the cow, which was extremely difficult.
They were not very rich people, and they had to make a living.
There was no pay, just food.
And very possibly that they did not-- although they suspected I'm Jewish, maybe they made believe they didn't know.
Because it is not easy to get a free mad.
I had not pleasant experiences, because the ones that were Ukrainian and their friends, they would say, [NON-ENGLISH].
This is a Jewish girl.
And they always blocked it out.
They never made the comment.
They did not answer one way or the other.
And they ignored her.
But she loved the little boys.
She was a single woman, an old one, looked like a witch.
And she said that she takes care so well of the children.
Who cares?
She would say.
My boss had a brother in the town of [INAUDIBLE]-- a brother with his wife and mother, and they were frequently visiting us with their two children.
The times when [INAUDIBLE] started to kill the Polish people, because they had a dream that they would get-- from the Germans, they would get an independent Ukraine after the war.
And they figured if they do away with the Poles, they'll have less of a problem to accomplish that goal.

And this mother of my boss visited, and she told me that she knows I'm Jewish, she has a feeling I'm Jewish, and I should not tell the ones that I do not trust, but she's going to tell-- if I want to, I can tell myself to my boss, to her son, and to her other son.
And I did.
They really did not comment one way or the other.
It was harvest time, and we helped out other families in the neighborhood-- Polish families that had also farms.
And as I was helping with the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't know how the [NON-ENGLISH] is in English-- lifting the wheat.
You know, they showed me how to do it.
At times I would see they were snickering, like they knew that I wasn't meant to do the job, that I wasn't doing the job in the past.
We heard the shots and shouts, and that was an action in Miedzyrzecz, in the town.
Since we were high up, we could see.
We did not see the graves, we did not see, but we saw lots of running around and shooting.
They observed me, you know, like from the corner of their eyes.
And I made no comment.
And I just cried for them.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And when the harvest was over, and it was late fall already, we had a terrible--
Still '42.
We heard the terrible news that the family of my boss was killed during the night.
His brother, his wife, his mother and the children.
And he was devastated.
And after the funerals, you know, he decided that we don't sleep anymore at home, but we go to the church every night with the children, with the bedding and some food.
And the church was in the middle of town of Miedzyrzecz.
They were Poles, as I told you.
The [INAUDIBLE] did it.
The Germans did not even lift a finger to find out who were the killers.
They have not done a thing about it.
In the church.
Every night, like towards the dusk, we would collect our belongings, some of it, some food, and sleep in the church, and then come mornings back, take care of the cow, take her to pasture.
And I always-- when I went to the pastures, I always avoided the other Ukrainians that were there.
And there were mainly Ukrainians in the pastures.
So they would not ask me questions, or they would not have any conversations with me.
Only once, I met a schoolmate.
She was Czech, and she was under way from Miedzyrzecz to her town.
We had lots of Czech families there, which I am sure Jewish people maybe survived in their homes.
They were more friendly to the Jewish people, more friendly than the Ukrainians.
And I--
When I met her, she stopped.
She wanted to talk to me, but I did not-- but I turned around and I did not want to say anything to her.
And she could not-- even if she wanted to call authorities, she couldn't, because she wouldn't know where I lived, where I was residing.
And they wouldn't just go out and look for a girl that was in the pastures.
He did not want anymore to stay in the Miedzyrzecz.
He was much too afraid, and he has decided to move to the town of Rowne, which had no Ukrainians around, had more Poles.
And we packed up and moved.
Her parents did not move, her parents.
He was Ukrainian.
And he felt safe in town.
His wife was Polish, the children are considered were Polish.
So I moved with them to the town of Rowne.
And the-- [INAUDIBLE] and of course the boss and the two little ones, we all moved.
And [INAUDIBLE] also moved to the town of Rowne.
We had an apartment, and he found a job.
He was working days.
And then we had a census.
And in the census, he heard that the young people will be transported to Germany for work.
So he made sure that-- he made my age, instead of 1925, 1927.
So two years younger.
I was not not going to be sent to work.
Although there was not much to do in the apartment, but still I stayed with them.
And he had a notice that he has to go to the-- to dig the trenches.
That was already '44.
Time flies.
And the Russians were advancing.
So on the outskirts of Rowne, trenches were being dug.
So he had to go to work, so he sent me instead to do the digging of the trenches, and I did.
And I met Germans.
No problem, as long as a body shows up to do the work.
I was surrounded by Germans and Ukrainians.
But they paid no attention to who I was.
They assumed I'm not Jewish.
I mean, there were no Jewish left.
There was [NON-ENGLISH] already in all the towns of [INAUDIBLE], all the towns of [INAUDIBLE] and Ukraine.
And up north, and up south.
So they did not suspect I was Jewish.
I did my work.
And that is the time when I took very sick, apparently with pneumonia pleurisy.
And since the bombing started and the Russians were advancing, we all hid in a basement.
And there was no doctor to be gotten.
And there was no-- no doctor could come [INAUDIBLE].
I had high fever, and I was-- you know, that was it.
I was dying.
But the end-- Was there enough food?
There was enough food.
There was enough food.
You mean during that time when we had to hide already from the bombs?
I suppose so.
I suppose so.
And the [INAUDIBLE] said that she's going to help me, she's going to do copping.
Do you know what that is?
When you put-- copping is a little container, a glass container.
You put fire on the wood with alcohol, and you put it on the back and on the chest.
Apparently, that helped, because I survived, survived whether it was pneumonia or pleurisy, no one-- I couldn't remember.
And sure enough, that we heard that Hoszcza is freed, and that Miedzyrzecz is freed.
And they heard it from my-- from [INAUDIBLE] family, from my boss's family, that the Soviets are there.
The Soviets are there.
And when was this?
That was '44, in the winter of '44.
And then we-- we stayed in Rowne for awhile.
I was told that there is a Jewish woman that lives in Rowne that came together with the army.
And since it was the Soviet government already, and I wasn't afraid to walk the streets, I came to see her.
And she told me that she survived.
She's the only one.
And she survived close to Hoszcza, and she is now with the army.
While they were advancing, she came to the town of Rowne thinking that maybe some of her family she will find in Rowne, or in the towns near Rowne.
She was looking for them.
She told me that a few of the family survived, that she does not know anything about my mother or sister.
And that she knows that the pharmacist is alive, and his wife.
Yeah.
And I said to my-- to the family, I said that I'd like to go back to town.
They really tried to talk me out of it, and they said that I would be better off staying with them.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
They all knew I was Jewish.
I-- I wanted to go back to Hoszcza.
I wanted to see who survived.
And of course, I didn't know that my sister perished.
And I hoped that she will be there in the same village that I left her, or coming back to Hoszcza.
I have a very memorable incident that happened in the church.

And when the Poles were hiding-- or at least at night, not to be found home for the Ukrainians to kill them-- they said that if I should-- if Hanka should not survive, I think it would be a good idea for her to speak to the Polish priest.
So he would convert her to Catholicism.
If she does go to-- if she does die, at least she wouldn't go to hell.
Oh yes.
Not to pray, but to sleep the nights, to--
So the-- I went to church with them every night, yes.
To sleep.
I went to see the priest.
He was there, with the parishioners of course.
And I told him that the family is insisting that he should convert me to Catholicism.
And I told him that I am Jewish, and if he-- what does he think?
And he said, I think that if anyone remains of your family, you are not of age.
And tell them-- to tell them that you are not taking on the Catholicism.
I will not give you the-- I will not make you Catholic unless I have permission from your family after the war.
And I told them so.
And they believed me.
And they did not bother me again.
